Typically, I would take the past 10 or 15 years' data, but the reason I’m taking data only for the past 5 years is because that is when the world became more cognizant of the importance of semiconductor chips and, by extension, ASML’s monopoly and its criticality in the semiconductor supply chain.

The below table gathers data on ASML’s current valuation multiples from the company’s valuation metrics page available on Seeking Alpha. Missing data marked with an asterisk was filled using S&P Capital IQ. I calculated the % upside/downside in the stock for each valuation multiple so that it would reach its 5-year average. I then took an average across the upside/downside for all valuation multiples, which gave us an average downside of 11.57%. Based on Friday’s closing price of $1,459.80, that gives us a price target of $1,291.

Risks

Export Controls – China Revenue

In all of the last three full years, China has accounted for at least 29% of ASML’s net system sales. In Q1 2026, that figure dropped to 19%. The decline began after U.S. restrictions on exports were broadened to include more fab locations, mostly in China. As a result, ASML’s net new system sales to China declined by nearly 10% in 2025 compared to the previous year. For 2026, I have taken the estimates based on an estimate of 38 billion euros in revenues (midpoint of the company’s guidance) and a 72% share of system sales in the overall sales mix, with China contributing 19% of system sales.

My estimates may end up on the higher side, and the real numbers might be much worse. In early April 2026. A bipartisan group of lawmakers in the United States introduced the Multilateral Alignment of Technology Controls on Hardware [MATCH] Act, intended to cut off China from access to tools and technologies required to manufacture their own semiconductor chips, which is critical because some of them may have dual use, i.e., civilian as well as military applications.

ASML has never sold EUV lithography technology machines to China but has continued to sell DUV lithography machines, which are inferior technology compared to EUV. Some of ASML’s DUV machines are subject to Dutch export licenses issued by the USA. The MATCH Act, if passed, would prohibit ASML from selling even the DUV machines to China, putting further downward pressure on the company’s revenue and future growth.

ASML expects China to account for 20% of its total sales in 2026, meaning approximately €7.6 billion, down from 33% in 2025, or approximately €10.8 billion. So even without this act, ASML will lose €3.2 billion in sales from China, and it could get much worse. This not only impacts revenue and growth prospects this year but also future growth prospects. In my opinion, with trade/soft war involving key materials and minerals continuing between the U.S. and China, there will likely be at least a quota system or limits on ASML’s sales to China, if not a complete restriction on DUV sales. I believe the market is overlooking this risk amid a market driven by Iran headlines.

Conclusion

I want to conclude this article by placing a Hold rating on ASML stock with a price target of $1,291. ASML’s guidance assumes nearly 20% revenue growth in H2, which I feel is a bit optimistic, and there is risk to the downside. The potential restriction on DUV sales to China also creates downside risk for the stock. However, factors such as operating leverage and the criticality of the company in the ongoing strong AI build-out provide a cushion to the downside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Credo's Optical Pivot Is Changing The Entire Narrative
data.description:
Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO) is no longer a story about a good quarter in the wider AI story. In earlier discussion, CRDO has outperformed the overall market and the next question is whether we have a case of good temporary demand or the initial phase in the buildout of the vertically integrated interconnect platforms. The DustPhotonics acquisition puts CRDO in a different space as it goes from being just component suppliers to becoming signal providers by virtue of having DSP, PICs, and system integration capabilities.

From Breakdown to Breakout

I believe that the fall in CRDO’s valuation to below $100 was driven by the change in narrative and not by any deterioration in quarterly results. While earnings were quite impressive, investors started pricing the scenario when the dominance of Credo in AECs would be only temporary, and the whole ecosystem of chipmakers was going to shift to silicon photonics and optical interconnects. The second part here is that momentum rotation into other stocks, such as those from Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI), Lumentum Holdings (LITE) and Coherent Corp (COHR), became obvious once the industry got confirmation regarding the role of optics in future AI scaling.

What made the selloff sharper was that CRDO’s growth, while explosive, was still concentrated as the top hyperscalers accounted for the major portion of CRDO's run-rate. Besides, operating costs surged to ~$77 million as the company ramped up its research and development activities. All things considered, CRDO stock price had already gone from perfect execution to "what if there is no smooth scalability."

The breakout above $170 is explained by a change in perception. The DustPhotonics acquisition up to ~$1.3 billion allowed the company to incorporate silicon photonics PIC technology into its product portfolio and plan to deliver $500 million+ in optical revenue by FY27. Now CRDO is present in all major components needed for a chip-based interconnect, including SerDes, DSP, AEC and photonics. Moreover, the revenue trajectory is expected to reach ~$1.3 billion+ by FY26 and grow >50% by FY27.

Why the DustPhotonics Acquisition Is About More Than Numbers

The headline terms are easy enough to grasp with $750 million in cash, about 0.92 million shares upfront and as much as 3.21 million additional shares tied to milestones. Credo anticipates the transaction's closing in the second quarter of 2026 and its non-GAAP EPS accretion in fiscal 2027.

At first glance, this seems like an example of a fast-growing company paying a hefty sum for increasing its optical presence in order to capture more business. In my opinion, there is a different interpretation: the management saw that their reliance on third parties for silicon photonics capabilities created serious restrictions to the company's future development.

What matters to me here is that DustPhotonics offers fabless silicon photonics PIC capabilities in 400G, 800G and 1.6T products, as well as a road map for further expansion to 3.2T technology. Moreover, Credo tied this asset directly to the development of ZeroFlap optical transceivers, near-port optics and co-packaged optics.

According to management, the company is counting on its ZeroFlap transceiver, optical DSP and silicon photonics solution line to generate over $500 million in optical revenue in fiscal 2027. Such statements after reporting on a quarter where electrical connectivity was dominant cannot be considered a diversification step.

Here comes a critical sentence: acquiring the necessary PIC capability should mitigate supply chain risk and improve cost structure going forward. This statement should not be overlooked, as there are many cases in the market where issues such as power, reliability, yields and deployment speed influence customer purchasing decisions. Therefore, being able to provide vertically integrated solutions may become the difference between being a component supplier and the core of the entire platform.

Valuation Has Become a Test for Credo's Credibility

CRDO is not an options play anymore at ~$174, its price is built around execution already having been demonstrated and maintained. Given expected revenue for fiscal year 2026 at ~$1.3-$1.35 billion this represents a forward P/S ratio of ~24x which, again, is pricey even among the sector but close to industry's leaders like Astera Labs (ALAB) and LITE.

The question isn't whether such a multiple is justified in and of itself, but rather the underlying assumptions one has to take into account here. Assuming that at this price point, the market believes in the long-term durability of current margins (approaching ~50% non-GAAP op margin), and the ability of the optical segment to ramp quickly enough to justify the ~$1.3 billion DustPhotonics investment, while reducing overall customer concentration risks at the same time.

On this basis, if we assume the company to be able to deliver >50% revenue growth going into fiscal year 2027 and optical revenue to exceed $500 million, the company's current valuation starts to make sense. However, I'd still describe it as very fragile and not forgiving. While the stock is resilient to execution headwinds, it is highly sensitive to any changes to the narrative, and will respond negatively to any signs that Credo fails to transform into an interconnect platform, specifically the optical segment.

Risks That Would Force Me to Reframe

While a certain slowdown in revenue is normal after such an incredible growth rate, the problem for the thesis will begin if the sales growth will continue despite worsening margins. In such a scenario, DustPhotonics' acquisition will not help Credo in expanding its portfolio as quickly as required and generating higher revenues from optical products. It may turn out that Credo paid for its optionality, whereas the market assumed that such development is inevitable anyway.

Secondly, customer concentration will be another problem to worry about. It is quite possible that in periods of rapid sales increase the influence of one or two big customers will become overwhelming, and it might cause Credo's future underperformance.

Other risks worth mentioning are lack of integration discipline in light of multiple activities. So, buying a small company with 70 employees that focuses on silicon photonics technology is not such a massive move, but it is a considerable strategic change for Credo at a very active moment of its development. If the company fails to execute properly and loses control over expenses, multiple investors will not forgive its current margin level.

Final Take

I am still bullish about Credo although not carelessly. From my perspective, it looks like a promising platform transition that is presented as a momentum stock. As such, the situation looks attractive but requires careful positioning. Therefore, I will remain constructive as long as optical products' sales growth begins to show positive trends.
